The chase starts with the hook echo:<[silence:100]> a radar signature where rain wraps around a tornado’s core, like a comma drawn in precipitation.<[silence:400]> Chasers track this shape while monitoring mesocyclones—rotating updrafts visible on Doppler radar as tight, spinning blobs.<[silence:400]> Speed matters:<[silence:100]> a tornado can form in under 10 minutes, so teams position miles ahead, using GPS waypoints to stay ahead of the storm’s 30–50 mph advance.<[silence:400]>

clean_story:
Dodging tornadoes. Hunting data.

Storm chaser tactics borrow their name from the act of pursuing storms—not to flee, but to intercept. Like anglers reading water currents to find fish, chasers read wind shifts, radar echoes, and cloud shapes to predict a storm’s next move.

The chase starts with the hook echo: a radar signature where rain wraps around a tornado’s core, like a comma drawn in precipitation. Chasers track this shape while monitoring mesocyclones—rotating updrafts visible on Doppler radar as tight, spinning blobs. Speed matters: a tornado can form in under 10 minutes, so teams position miles ahead, using GPS waypoints to stay ahead of the storm’s 30–50 mph advance.

Tools are precise. Mobile mesonets—trucks packed with weather sensors—measure temperature drops (often 10°F+ near tornadoes) and wind shifts. Drones capture real-time footage, while turtle probes (armored data pods) are dropped into a storm’s path to record pressure plummets (some exceeding 100 mb in seconds). The goal isn’t thrill; it’s data. A single successful intercept can refine warning times from 13 minutes (current average) to 20+.

Three related subjects are tornado alley patterns, Doppler radar limitations, and extreme weather drones.
